2002-10-01.title: Mexican Oil Contract Is Victory for President
2002-10-01.abstract: President Vicente Fox has won a major political battle with old Mexico, forcing the powerful union at the state-run oil giant, Pemex, to call off a strike that would have sent the nation reeling.     Now comes the larger struggle to make Pemex work. Hundreds of billions of dollars, not to mention Mr. Fox's political fortunes, are at stake.

2002-10-01.title: Meddlers in Provinces Rattle Russia's Fragile Election System
2002-10-01.abstract: Russian democracy appeared today to be stumbling after local authorities in two areas meddled with election results, causing a national scandal.     Radio talk shows, television news programs and newspaper headlines were all buzzing with the news from Krasnoyarsk, the Siberian region where the local election commission on Sunday unexpectedly annulled the results of a recent gubernatorial election.

2002-10-01.title: Qaeda Suspect Talking, U.S. Says
2002-10-01.abstract: Deputy Defense Secretary Paul D. Wolfowitz said today that Ramzi bin al-Shibh, the suspected member of Al Qaeda captured in Pakistan earlier this month, was providing American authorities with ''valuable information.''    Speaking at the inaugural meeting of the U.S.-Pakistan Business Council, Mr. Wolfowitz thanked the Pakistani government for seizing Mr. bin al-Shibh, who ''is not only off the streets now, but in fact is providing us with valuable information.''

2002-10-01.title: Europeans to Exempt U.S. From War Court
2002-10-01.abstract: The 15 nations of the European Union agreed today to exempt American soldiers and government officials from prosecution for war crimes at the International Criminal Court, an issue that had troubled trans-Atlantic relations for several months.    The compromise, reached at a meeting of European Union foreign ministers, came close to the blanket immunity for American government employees sought by the Bush administration, although European officials emphasized that in their view it did not undermine the court, which the administration has opposed.

2002-10-01.title: Sir John Rennie Is Dead at 85; Led Aid Effort for Palestinians
2002-10-01.abstract: Sir John Rennie, British administrator who struggled to keep United Nations aid program for Palestinian refugees afloat in 1960's and 70's, dies at 85; photo (M)

2002-10-02.title: Stable World Oil Prices Are Likely to Become a War Casualty, Experts Say
2002-10-02.abstract: Oil experts warns of severe disruption in world oil markets if military action is taken against Iraq; prices have already risen substantially and analysts warn that Iraq could dissolve into civil war if Saddam Hussein is ousted, disrupting flow of 1.1 million barrels a day; Bush administration and Western oil companies take rosier view, predicting that Iraqi reserves, second only to Saudi Arabia's, would flow with lifting of sanctions and influx of foreign investment; graphs (M)

2002-10-02.title: U.S. Faulted Over Its Efforts to Unite Iraqi Dissidents
2002-10-02.abstract: Bush administration is reportedly stumbling in efforts to forge cohesive opposition to Saddam Hussein, plagued by rivalries over who should lead dissidents and who would rule most effectively if Hussein were overthrown; brief summaries of key opposition groups; many are not organized strictly along ethnic or religious lines, but Kurdish and Shiite groups are concentrated in certain areas; photos of leaders; map (M)

2002-10-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Blood Tests For Unconscious Drivers
2002-10-02.abstract: New British law allows police to have doctors take blood samples from unconscious drivers after accidents; civil rights advocates protest (S)

2002-10-02.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-10-02.abstract: SOUTH KOREA: Protesters Enter U.S. Compound; INDIA: 14 Die As Kashmir Vote Continues; PHILIPPINES: Executions Suspended;.

2002-10-02.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Protesters Enter U.S. Compound
2002-10-02.abstract: Seven South Korean students scale wall of US Embassy in attempt to protest Pres Bush's failure to apologize for deaths of two schoolgirls crushed to death by US armored vehicle; police stop group; US officials have apologized and two Army sergeants face courts-martial in June accident (S)

2002-10-03.title: Iraqis Stall for Time, Playing Weak Hand Well
2002-10-03.abstract: Analysis: Iraq's decision to readmit United Nations inspectors heightens differences between United States and its allies and complicates Bush administration's task of winning Security Council approval for tough new resolution; Iraq's objective appears to be to ensure that inspection arrangements are as weak as possible before admitting international monitors, while acquiescing to inspections as way to forestall American attack; Iraq has taken weak hand and played it effectively (M)

2002-10-03.title: War, Murder and Suicide: A Year's Toll Is 1.6 Million
2002-10-03.abstract: Violence kills more than 1.6 million people each year, and suicide claims almost as many lives as war and homicide combined, according to a new report.

2002-10-03.title: New Details on Deaths of 8 Japanese Seized by North Korea
2002-10-03.abstract: Japanese diplomats return home from visit to North Korea and provide first detailed accounting of deaths of eight of their compatriots kidnapped by North Koreans beginning in late 1970's; say they were told that eight died of causes ranging from suicide to auto accidents and heart disease, and also told that ninth missing Japanese woman is alive and has been married to American military defector for two decades; say four additional abducted Japanese citizens found alive in North Korea expressed eagerness to see Japanese relatives but have reservations about being separated from their North Korean spouses and families; diplomats' new information in 13 cases is greeted with widespread disbelief in Japan, especially about those reported to be dead; news of American military defector provides little more detail than is already known by US government about Charles Robert Jenkins, who defected in 1960's; photo (M)

2002-10-03.title: France and Germany Agree on Iraq
2002-10-03.abstract: Leaders of France and Germany, meeting in Paris, say they oppose any effort to alter conditions under which United Nations inspectors operate; Pres Jacques Chirac and Chancellor Gerhard Schroder say they are 'totally hostile' to any UN resolutions that would give automatic permission for military intervention; Bush administration is irked by Paris's insistence that there be two resolutions, one demanding Iraqi compliance with UN inspectors, and second spelling out consequences, including strikes, if Baghdad fails to meet demands; photo (M)

2002-10-03.title: Nervousness from war talk contributes to the worst quarter in U.S. since 1987 : The global sell-off extends to Japan
2002-10-03.abstract: While stock prices rebounded in most of the world, Japanese shares sank to a 19-year low Wednesday on concerns that the government's cleanup of bad loans could prompt the collapse of some listed companies.

2002-10-04.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Makes Florida Look Good
2002-10-04.abstract: Pres Vladimir V Putin appoints Aleksandr Khloponin acting governor of Krasnoyarsk, Siberia (S)

2002-10-04.title: New Role for U.S. in Colombia: Protecting a Vital Oil Pipeline
2002-10-04.abstract: United States Special Forces will train Colombian soldiers in counterinsurgency to protect 500-mile pipeline snaking through eastern Colombia that carries crude to oil-hungry America; it is one of country's most valuable assets and has long been vulnerable to bombings by country's guerrilla groups; it transports 100,000 barrels of oil a day for Occidental Petroleum of Los Angeles; its protection dovetails with Bush administration's new, global emphasis on expanding and diversifying sources of America's oil imports, with eye to reducing dependence on Middle Eastern oil; $94 million counterinsurgency program is also important element in offensive by Colombia's new government against two rebel groups and paramilitary force that dominate much of country; pipeline bombings by guerrillas cost government nearly $500 million last year; photo; map; chart (M)

2002-10-04.title: Pakistanis Are Skeptical of Musharraf's Promises
2002-10-04.abstract: Government of Gen Pervez Musharraf is presenting upcoming parliamentary elections in Pakistan as means to restore almost total civilian rule, but most Pakistanis and international election-watchers see major flaws; note leaders of two of three major parties are living in exile, and that government order has disqualified any candidate who lacks bachelor's degree, thus barring 90 percent of population, as well as 30 percent of last Parliament; in addition, Musharraf has decreed that he could dissolve Parliament and amend Constitution at any time, and that he will not be bound by any ministerial advice; in Lahore, epicenter of Pakistani politics, there is little to suggest that national election is nearing climax; some parties are said to be thinking of boycotting at last moment; Musharraf himself is not on ballot, having won five-year term in referendum that opponents charge was boycotted by 75 percent of Pakistanis; photo (M)

2002-10-04.title: Dubai Journal; Living High and Aiming Higher, Come War or Peace
2002-10-04.abstract: Dubai, city-state that once lived off duty-free goods and trade, has reinvented itself; has transformed its skyline with innovative architecture and now promotes itself as financial and transportation hub; unlike other members of United Arab Emirates, Dubai is thriving, focusing its energies not so much on politics as on making and spending of money; photo of its most talked-about new building, luxury hotel shaped like full-blown spinnaker, with royal suites going for $8,000 a night; map (M)

2002-10-04.title: The Collections / Paris: From Galliano, hard core Dior
2002-10-04.abstract: PARIS—  'Gold power!" said John Galliano as he was snapped backstage by an ecstatic Penelope Cruz while fellow actresses Christina Ricci and Rosanna Arquette joined the congratulatory throng at Dior's spring/summer show Thursday.

2002-10-05.title: Using a Peaceful Time to Reclaim a Painful Past
2002-10-05.abstract: Profile of Krzysztof Czyzewski, Polish intellectual, who leads group that seeks to reclaim past, bad and good, for Poland; he spent years under Communism as underground theater director and performer with interest in Poland's cultural traditions; last year he helped set off one of most searing recent debates in Poland, when his group's publishing house printed book that alleges that it was Poles, not occupying Nazi troops, who killed 1,600 Jews in 1941 in village of Jedwabne; book ultimately led Pres Aleksander Kwasniewski to apologize on behalf of Poles; photo (M)

2002-10-05.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-10-05.abstract: NORTH KOREA: American Sees No. 2 Official;.

2002-10-05.title: More Young Victims Falling On Front Lines in the Mideast
2002-10-05.abstract: Current Palestinian uprising enters its third year with Israeli forces reoccupying most West Bank cities; children are often on front line as soldiers use live ammunition to enforce curfews and break up violent street protests, sometimes with deadly results; in most recent incident, soldiers open fire during stone-throwing clash at village near Jenin, killing 15-year-old boy; photo (M)

2002-10-05.title: Muslim Rebels Are Blamed For Bombing In Philippines
2002-10-05.abstract: Pentagon and Philippine officials say Abu Sayyaf guerrillas were probably responsible for explosion in Zamboanga that killed American Green Beret Mark Wayne Jackson, seriously wounded another American soldier, killed two Filipinos and injured 20 others; just two months ago United States ended counterterrorism training mission in Philippines by declaring that Abu Sayyaf had been routed; photo; map (M)

2002-10-05.title: Belfast Police, in Unusual Move, Raid Sinn Fein Office and Homes
2002-10-05.abstract: Police raid legislative offices of Sinn Fein, political wing of Irish Republican Army, and homes of party supporters in series of operations across Belfast; four people are arrested and documents and computer disks are seized; operations follows yearlong investigation into whether IRA infiltrated Northern Ireland Office, Britain's headquarters in province; police suspect that former messenger in office routinely intercepted and photocopied mail and passed it on to Sinn Fein or IRA; messenger is arrested, as is Denis Donaldson, Sinn Fein's chief administrator for provincial parliament; arrests and raids may lead Britain to reinstate direct rule from London; subject is likely to be major agenda item at upcoming meeting between Prime Min Bertie Ahern of Irish Republic and Prime Min Tony Blair (M)

2002-10-06.title: Rejecting Boycott, Researchers Gather in Israel
2002-10-06.abstract: Group of medical researchers from Columbia University, saying they are taking stand against academic boycotts of Israeli institutions, organize scientific conference this weekend in Eilat, Israel; conference, Frontiers in Cardiovascular Science, is featuring speakers from US, Canada and Europe; aims to share research findings and to send broader message of solidarity to Israeli scholars, who have found themselves isolated in recent months by boycott protesting Israel's presence in West Bank and Gaza Strip; boycott began with petitions drawn up by British neurochemist Steven Rose (M)

2002-10-06.title: Hopeful Germans Strain to Read Between Lines of Bush Letter
2002-10-06.abstract: Letter sent by Pres Bush to German Pres Johannes Rau, wishing Germany well on 12th anniversary of Germany's reunification, is seen by many Germans as attempt at reconciliation between two nations since Bush administration snubbed Chancellor Gerhard Schroder's election last month, in part by campaigning on his opposition to American-led war against Iraq; Bush administration officials dismiss letter as routine communication between governments and say it does not represent attempt at reconciliation; Rau's spokesman Klaus Schrotthofer says letter has unmistakably conciliatory tone; German officials have tried to improve relations with US since election (M)

2002-10-06.title: Nigerian Militias Wield Power Through Intimidation
2002-10-06.abstract: Nigerian militias are wielding power through intimidation; political killings are on rise with elections coming up next year; Bakassi Boys, one of country's most notorious militias in Anambra State, was legalized in August 2000, and salaries o fits members have been paid from public money; Bakassi Boys, whose official name is Anambra State Vigilante Services and whose members belong to main ethnic group of region, Ibos, have office inside governor's headquarters in state capital Awka; recent killings of dissident lawyers Barnabas and Amaka Blessing Igwe in Onitsha, in state of Anambra, detailed; map; photos (M)

2002-10-06.title: In China, Catholic Churches Flourish, but Under Controls
2002-10-06.abstract: Many Roman Catholic churches are thriving in China, but under government control; Beizhan's new church has finally replaced small one destroyed during antireligious fervor of Cultural Revolution of late 1960's; 1,500 of Beizhan's 5,000 residents attend services on typical Sunday, with up to 3,000 on holy days like Christmas; remarkable renaissance has occurred in construction of Catholic churches and in charitable works in China in recent years; behind this development is resurgence of faith, relaxation of government policy toward officially registered religious groups and better access to money from international Catholic organizations; changes have heightened tensions between 'official' churches, which register with government and allow Communist Party to govern church affairs, and clandestine 'underground' churches, which cling to supremacy of pope at cost of persecution; only official churches are allowed to accept foreign money; experts say there are about 12 million practicing Catholics in China, number that by all accounts is growing rapidly; map; photos (M)

2002-10-06.title: Brazil May Not Stay Upright on a Shaky Global Stage
2002-10-06.abstract: Analysis: No matter who wins Brazilian presidential elections, biggest loser could end up being American recipe for globalization; few countries have won more praise on economic policy than Brazil, from Bush administration and International Monetary Fund; few are being punished as harshly as Brazil by foreign investors, who have been running for exits for months; Brazil's currency has slipped to record lows and its government bonds are selling for about 53 percent on dollar in anticipation that left-wing candidate Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva will have big first-round victory; most of fear is that da Silva will relax current policies to cut spending, fight inflation and keep paying on Brazil's $240 billion public debt; growing number of experts fear that Brazil will unravel even if da Silva keeps his recent pledge to hold down spending; US believes Brazil can weather its crisis regardless of who wins (M)

2002-10-07.title: The Collections/Paris : Glorious Gaultier, brilliant Balenciaga, magnificent McQueen
2002-10-07.abstract: PARIS—  Three exceptional shows marked Paris fashion's long weekend. Jean Paul Gaultier defined Gallic sensuality in a witty show on a romantic theme. At Balenciaga, Nicolas Ghesquiere made wetsuits, surfing and water sports the height of cool. And Alexander McQueen unrolled a narrative saga along with beautiful clothes.

2002-10-07.title: Rabbi Zorach Warhaftig Dies; Rescuer of Polish Jews Was 96
2002-10-07.abstract: Rabbi Zorach Warhaftig, who helped save several thousand Jews during World War II, dies at age 96; photo (M)

2002-10-07.title: Afghans in the Northeast Enjoy a Controlled Calm
2002-10-07.abstract: Life in Kunduz, in far northeastern corner of Afghanistan, is most peaceful and stable in region, which is in danger of being forgotten because there has been no recent fighting, ethnic tensions or attacks; four provinces--Kunduz, Baghlan, Takhar, Badakshan--are well ahead of others in disarming after years of warfare; ethnic Pashtun population, who were largely supporters of Taliban, have not been targets of reprisals as have other groups elsewhere; various ethnic groups--Pashtuns, Tajiks, Turkmens, Uzbeks--have settled back into old pattern of life; main reason for stability is that one party, Tajik-led Northern Alliance, controls northeast region; map; photo (M)

2002-10-07.title: Youth mix and match styles across borders and eras : Japan's anything-goes mentality
2002-10-07.abstract: TOKYO—  As she wondered what to wear to a recent outdoor festival featuring rock, funk, reggae and rave artists, Ami Ishimatsu found something appropriate, for contemporary Japan at least.

2002-10-07.title: For Afghan Central Bank, It's Out With the Old Money and In With the New
2002-10-07.abstract: Afghanistan's central bank in capital of Kabul is astir under American-backed government of Hamid Karzai after years of degeneration under Taliban, who looted vaults at gunpoint under pressure of American bombing before fleeing in November 2001, leaving nation of 25 million with only $30,000 in cash reserves; has first computers linked by satellite to world's financial markets as well as new, economically literate management aided by American financial advisers; is seeking to remake itself into institution with weight across Afghanistan, instead of relic it became in years when war and lawlessness voided very notion of effective central bank; will start by issuing new currency on October 7 to replace estimated 15 trillion old afghani notes; photos (M)

2002-10-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Plan For Tighter Asylum Rules
2002-10-08.abstract: Britain proposes that asylum claims from ten countries expected to join European Union by 2004 be automatically presumed unfounded because those countries are safe (S)

2002-10-08.title: Court to Rule on Dutch Nurse Accused in 13 Deaths
2002-10-08.abstract: Trial of Lucy de Berk, 41-year-old Dutch nurse charged with killing 13 patients by injecting them with lethal cocktails of drugs and of trying to kill five others, rivets residents of the Hague; widely reported as largest serial murder case ever seen in Netherlands, it has also touched nerve in nation that is first to legalize euthanasia and doctor-assisted suicide; prosecutors accuse her of being classic psychopath and serial killer, despite professed love for critically ill patients, and are using her personal diaries as evidence at trial, under way since September (M)

2002-10-08.title: In Olive Groves of the West Bank, a Harvest of Fear
2002-10-08.abstract: Conflict between Israel and Palestinians in West Bank casts shadow over harvesting of olives as picking season begins; has made it harder in many places to collect fruit; Palestinians are unable to pick olives from their groves that abut settlements because armed settlers have kept them away; accuse settlers of theft; settlers cite need to keep potential attackers away; say they are taking no chances after two years of Palestinian assaults on their communities; killing of Hani Bani Minyeh by settlers in Aqraba while working in olive grove described; photo (M)

2002-10-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Putin Less Hawkish On Georgia
2002-10-08.abstract: Russian Pres Vladimir Putin softens tons on Georgia after meeting with Pres Eduard Shevardnadze in Moldova and agreeing to conduct joint patrols along Chechnya border (S)

2002-10-08.title: Ulster Leader Urges Blair to Oust Sinn Fein From Parliament
2002-10-08.abstract: David Trimble, the first minister of the Northern Ireland Assembly, called on Prime Minister Tony Blair today to evict Sinn Fein.

2002-10-09.title: Israel Defiantly Warns of More Antiterror Raids in Gaza
2002-10-09.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon, shouldering aside Bush administration criticism, warns of more Israeli raids into Gaza to prevent terrorist attacks; skirmishes are now daily affair on margins of Gaza, Hamas stronghold; photo; Hamas is also pressed for second day by Palestinian Authority over killing of riot police chief Rajeh Abu Lehiya, apparently in revenge for death of Hamas figure last year (M)

2002-10-09.title: Big luxury labels are losing cachet
2002-10-09.abstract: LONDON—  It used to be that fashion buyers could carry on like Texan businessmen on a Las Vegas weekend. Their idea of a buying appointment would be to slap down $35,000 on Armani (or the label of the day) as if they were putting stacks of chips on black, which given the limited color palette of the early '90s was often quite literally the case.

2002-10-09.title: The Collections / PARIS: Sex vs. sport:Ford’s YSL is surreal
2002-10-09.abstract: PARIS—  Sex and sport are this millennium’s bread and circuses —and fashion has followedsuit. In the spring/summer 2003 shows,the battlebetween the stiletto and the sneaker was played out between Yves Saint Laurent, where Tom Ford had a Freudian fashion moment; and Yohji Yamamoto, who rolled out his firstY3 collaboration with Adidas.

2002-10-09.title: 2 new books scrutinize a traditional Gallic obsession : Why France disdains America
2002-10-09.abstract: PARIS:—  Two new books by French authors, one at the top of the best-seller list, the other described as a work of exceptional scholarship, are confronting the French with the proposition that their anti-Americanism is a self-inflicted national illness.

2002-10-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Suspect In Girls' Killing To Stand Trial
2002-10-09.abstract: British school custodian Ian Huntley has been ruled mentally fit to stand trial for murder of 10-year-old girls Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman (S)

2002-10-10.title: House Passes Iraq Resolution With 296 to 133 Vote
2002-10-10.abstract: The House voted to authorize President Bush to use force against Iraq, while the Senate gave its own show of support by clearing the way for the measure's final passage.

2002-10-10.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Ex-Oil Chief Arrested
2002-10-10.abstract: Rogelio Montemayor, former director of Mexico's state oil company, is arrested in Houston and faces extradition on charges of funneling tens of millions of dollars from Pemex to Institutional Revolutionary Party during 2000 presidential campaign (S)

2002-10-10.title: The Collections / Paris : An exceptional season offers imagination and creativity
2002-10-10.abstract: PARIS—  A monumental Paris season is winding down and spring/summer 2003 will remain in the collective fashion memory as the strongest in at least a decade. There has been an overall dynamism and energy not seen in fashion — meaning clothing — since the handbag took over.

2002-10-10.title: Social responsibility is becoming a major selling point : Making clothes with a conscience
2002-10-10.abstract: NEW YORK—  Ruth Meza-Orozco, 23, works six days a week in a Taiwanese clothing factory in Managua's Free Trade Zone. On Sundays, she studies. "My salary doesn't help me with anything," said Meza-Orozco, who has been working in the Free Trade Zone since she was 18. "I don't even have a house."

2002-10-10.title: World Briefing | Africa: Sudan: Goverment Agrees To Resume Talks
2002-10-10.abstract: Sudan agrees to return to peace talks that were halted following rebel military gains in south (S)

2002-10-11.title: Court Rules for Cameroon In Oil Dispute With Nigeria
2002-10-11.abstract: World Court rules at Hague that Cameroon is rightful owner of oil-rich Bakassi Peninsula in Gulf of Guinea that has long been claimed by Nigeria; decision in longstanding dispute could shuffle some of oil wealth in area thought to hold huge offshore deposits; dispute has limited exploration, but peninsula's proximity to other abundant fields holds promise of deposits of hundreds of millions of barrels; this would be timely boost for Cameroon, whose production has been steadily declining; map (M)

2002-10-11.title: Pakistani Fundamentalists and Other Opponents of Musharraf Do Well in Elections
2002-10-11.abstract: Both Islamic fundamentalists and secular opponents of Pres Pervez Musharraf appear to fare well in national elections in Pakistan; early returns indicate divided legislature; vote could complicate American-led campaign against terrorism; coalition of six fundamentalist religious parties running on vehemently anti-American platforms appear headed for their strongest showing ever, winning as many as 30 seats and making them third or forth largest party in assembly; results, if they hold up, are major setback for Musharraf, who has cracked down on militant Islamic groups; photo (M)

2002-10-11.title: Nobel Peace Prize Awarded to Carter With Criticism of Bush
2002-10-11.abstract: Jimmy Carter's selection "must be interpreted as a criticism of the present U.S. administration," the committee chair said.

2002-10-11.title: Police Station Blast Kills 9 in Chechnya
2002-10-11.abstract: Powerful explosion tears through police station in Grozny, Chechen capital, killing at least nine people and burying many under rubble; Grozny prosecutor's office says there appears to have been bomb, but other officials say they have not ruled out possibility of gas explosion (S)

2002-10-11.title: Rome Journal; Pinocchio Infatuates Italians (and That's No Lie)
2002-10-11.abstract: Italians are infatuated with Pinocchio, helped by Hollywood-style hoopla preceding release of new, live-action movie of Pinocchio by Italian superstar Roberto Benigni; latex Pinocchio noses and Pinocchio backpacks are everywhere, along with Pinocchio dolls; Pinocchio is even being feminized, with introduction of Pinocchia, book giving Pinocchio a soul mate and exploring passion between puppets; photos (M)

2002-10-12.title: Pentagon Aide, a Cuban Spy, Is Described as Unapologetic
2002-10-12.abstract: Federal prosecutors say in court documents that Ana B Montes, high-ranking Pentagon intelligence analyst who spied for Cuba because she opposes American policies toward Latin America, 'in no measure apologizes for her betrayal of the United States'; Montes pleaded guilty to espionage last March, acknowledging that for 16 years she provided Fidel Castro's government with top-secret information, including true identities of four American undercover intelligence officers; prosecutors are satisfied with her cooperation during debriefings (M)

2002-10-12.title: 5 Little Oryxes and the Big Bad Lioness of Kenya
2002-10-12.abstract: Lioness in Samburu National Reserve in northern Kenya astonishes naturalists by adopting baby oryxes; sight of full-grown lion at side of newborn antelope attracts hordes of tourists; photo; map (M)

2002-10-12.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: 4 Christians Sent To Labor Camp
2002-10-12.abstract: Four women who helped publish magazine for banned South China Church, underground Christian church, are sent without trial to 're-education through labor' camp for three years (S)

2002-10-12.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-10-12.abstract: CHINA: 4 Christians Sent To Labor Camp; JAPAN: Death Sentence For Nerve Gas Attack;.

2002-10-12.title: A Writer Who Followed His Own Clues to Fame
2002-10-12.abstract: Profile of and interview with Italian writer Andrea Camilleri; photo (M)

2002-10-13.title: Iraq Tour of Suspected Sites Gives Few Clues on Weapons
2002-10-13.abstract: Journalists tour industrial plant in Al Furat, Iraq, identified by Pres Bush as one of sites of past nuclear programs being rebuilt by Pres Saddam Hussein and leave with few clues as to plant's real purpose; tour is marked by ambiguities and frustrations; Iraqi officers in charge flatly deny plant has anything to do with nuclear technology, but realities glimpsed by visitors left many with sense that they may have been less than candid; facility described; maps; photo (M)

2002-10-13.title: Kenya's Leader to Step Down but Not Out
2002-10-13.abstract: Kenya Pres Daniel arap Moi, one of Africa's last remaining domineering leaders, prepares to step down; virtually all Kenyans agree that country that Moi will leave behind is both deeply flawed and inextricably linked to man who has run it since 1978; protests have become commonplace in waning days of Moi era; elections are scheduled for Dec; Moi is leaving on his own terms; has chosen as his successor Uhuru Kenyatta, 41-year-old son of country's founding president, Jomo Kenyatta; some of Moi's top lieutenants, including his vice pres, George Saitoti, have declared their own presidential bids; Moi has responded by firing dissenters and working aggressively behind the scenes to stage-manage Kenyatta victory; photos; map (M)

2002-10-13.title: 2nd Iraq Letter Stops Short of Reconfirming Pacts
2002-10-13.abstract: Iraq sends second, more conciliatory letter to chief United Nations weapons inspectors, but once again stops short of fully reconfirming agreements two sides made earlier in week; Amir Hammudi Al-Saadi, adviser to Saddam Hussein, sends letter to Hans Blix, head of United Nations weapons inspection team; photo of members of Iraq's Parliament (M)

2002-10-13.title: Serbia's Runoff Candidates Fight Voter Apathy
2002-10-13.abstract: Voter apathy may prevent Vojislav Kostunica, the nationalist who in his job as president of Yugoslavia has criticized free market reforms, from becoming the Serbian president.

2002-10-13.title: The French Spar Over Sex: There's a Limit, No?
2002-10-13.abstract: France, nation that has long cherished eroticism and abhorred censorship, is struggling with issues like how much hard-core pornography to allow on television and whether to permit pediophiliac acts in its novels; new moralistic wave coincides with election of center-right government of Pres Jacques Chirac, which has reinforced desire to preserve, or perhaps restore, what Americans have long called 'family values;' prompted recent demonstrations by feminists, gays and prostitutes in Paris, where they denounced 'return to moral order''; photo (M)

2002-10-14.title: Sharon Calls On Palestinians to Change Their 'Murderous Regime'
2002-10-14.abstract: As he prepared to leave Israel for a meeting with President Bush, Prime Minister Ariel Sharon called on Palestinians today to change their "murderous regime," saying that the coming year could be "a year of change."

2002-10-14.title: At Cuba Conference, Old Foes Exchange Notes on 1962 Missile Crisis
2002-10-14.abstract: American and Soviet protagonists in Cuban missile crisis of 1962, which brought world to brink of nuclear war, meet for first time, Havana, for conference; reminisce about events, discuss issues that arose from crisis, as well as its possible lessons for today; conference is attended by Pres Fidel Castro; American delegation is led by Robert S McNamara, defense secretary during Kennedy administration; conference was organized by Cuban government and American research group National Security Archive; photos (M)

2002-10-14.title: Low Turnout Invalidates the Results of Serbia's Election
2002-10-14.abstract: More than half of Serbia's electorate fails to vote in presidential election, weary of political infighting and unsure of which reformist faction to support, rendering result invalid; only 45.4 percent casts ballots, ignoring warnings from both presidential candidates that failed election would only drag out political instability holding back nation's economic recovery; results show incumbent conservative nationalist Vojislav Kostunica, who succeeded Slobodan Milosevic, easily defeated Miroljub Labus, pro-Western Liberal and architect of government's economic reform program; law requires that elections be held again within two months if less than 50 percent of voters turn out (M)

2002-10-14.title: Indonesia Blames Al Qaeda for Bali Bombing
2002-10-14.abstract: Indonesia's defense minister linked Al Qaeda today to the Saturday blast that killed and injured hundreds of people in Bali.

2002-10-14.title: Bombing in Bali Seen as Opening New Front in Fight on Terror
2002-10-14.abstract: Western officials say bombing of popular tourist discotheques in Bali, Indonesia, that killed nearly 200 people is different type of terrorism from what Bush administration has campaigned against and will open new geographic front in struggle; target was not American Embassy, military outpost or financial institution that would represent American power, of sort that terrorists have attacked in past; was nightclub whose revelers were mostly Australians and Europeans; attack puts intense pressure on Indonesian government to face terrorist threat at home more seriously; confirms warnings by American and Singaporean officials, ignored by Indonesia, that Al Qaeda cells were hiding in Indonesia, Muslim nation with porous borders and weak law enforcement; is condemned by Pres Bush as 'heinous' and Australian Prime Min John Howard as 'act of barbarity;' is one of deadliest on civilians anywhere in world in last decade and seems intended to undercut feelings of safety, even in remote enclave; occurred on faraway island, primarily populated by Hindus, with reputation for tranquility and popular as resort with backpackers and wealthy alike; State Dept orders all dependents of American diplomats to leave country, along with all nonessential personnel (M)

2002-10-15.title: Abducted a Lifetime Ago, 5 Japanese Come Home for a Visit
2002-10-15.abstract: Five middle-aged men and women stepped off an airplane today and into a Japan they had not known in a quarter of a century.

2002-10-15.title: F.B.I. Aids Pakistan in Arrest of 4 Afghans Linked to Qaeda
2002-10-15.abstract: The Pakistani police, accompanied by F.B.I. agents, arrested four Afghan men suspected of ties to Al Qaeda.

2002-10-15.title: Al Qaeda Evolves Into Looser Network, Experts Say
2002-10-15.abstract: Intelligence and law enforcement officials in US and elsewhere are redefining thinking about Al Qaeda and threat it represent since rout of organization by US forces in Afghanistan year ago because followers have since dispersed throughout world to establish themselves within loosely knit alliance of like-minded but independent groups (M)

2002-10-15.title: Sharon Urges Palestinians to Replace 'Murderous Regime'
2002-10-15.abstract: Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon, on eve of visit to Washington, calls on Palestinians to change 'murderous regime,' saying coming year could be 'year of change;' Muhammad Dahlin, Palestinian promoted by Israeli and American officials as possible alternative to Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, resigns as Arafat adviser; resignation comes as Arafat forms new government under pressure from Palestinian Parliament; Dahlin's resignation year ago, which was rejected by Arafat, noted; photos (M)

2002-10-15.title: Buenos Aires aims to cut dropout rate
2002-10-15.abstract: BUENOS AIRES—  In an attempt to halt a steep decline in educational standards and a massive dropout rate from high schools, the city of Buenos Aires in September became the first region in Latin America to make at least 12 years of education compulsory for all.

2002-10-16.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-10-16.abstract: UKRAINE: Judge Orders Inquiry Of President; DENMARK: Anger Over Cheese Ruling; ITALY: Ex-Guard Kills Seven;.

2002-10-16.title: With Only One Choice, Iraqis Give Hussein a New Term
2002-10-16.abstract: In a result that was a foregone conclusion, Saddam Hussein was overwhelmingly returned to power as president of Iraq, a top official in Baghdad said today.

2002-10-16.title: World Briefing | Asia: Taiwan: Missile Officer Apparently Defects
2002-10-16.abstract: Taiwan officials issue arrest warrant for Lieutenant Wang I-hung, who is in Taiwan's missile command; he apparently defected to China during vacation with his family (S)

2002-10-16.title: Tel Aviv Journal; Once Taboo, a Gay Israeli Treads the Halls of Power
2002-10-16.abstract: Interview with Uzi Even, 62-year-old chemistry professor and retired Israeli army officer who will be sworn in as first openly gay member of Israeli Parliament; will represent leftist Meretz Party, succeeding retiring Amnon Rubenstein; photo (M)

2002-10-16.title: Beijing Says Chinese Muslims Are Among Detainees in Cuba
2002-10-16.abstract: Chinese Deputy Prime Min Qian Qichen says US is holding more than dozen Uighur Muslims from western China, seized in Afghanistan, at its terrorist detention center at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba (S)

2002-10-17.title: Havana Enshrines Heroes of Espionage
2002-10-17.abstract: Case of five Cubans convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage in United States provides Cuban government with its latest ideological battleground against US; they have been made focus of rallies, televised discussions and appeals to international community; diplomats say it is proving difficult to generate much sympathy for men who admitted to having worked for Cuban intelligence; photos (M)

2002-10-17.title: Indonesia Links Muslim  Group With Terrorism
2002-10-17.abstract: Indonesian government, in about-face, acknowledges that fundamentalist Islamic group Jemaah Islamiyah exists and is terrorist organization, and that its leader may be Abu Bakar Bashir, Islamic preacher who expresses admiration for Osama bin Laden and loathing for Jews and West; several foreign governments suspect group was involved in blast in Bali; Indonesian government is under pressure from United States to act decisively against terrorism; Indonesia's State Security Minister Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono stops short of saying Bashir will be arrested, as US and other countries have requested; Yudhoyono says for first time publicly that Hambali, another Indonesian cleric, is one of Jemaah Islamiyah's leaders; Asian and Western intelligence agencies say it was Hambali who allied Jemaah Islamiyah with Al Qaeda in 1990's; Philippine officials say they will indict Hambali, who has been fugitive for many months, on charges of illegal possession of explosives that were intended for attacks on American and Israeli Embassies in Singapore that were thwarted by Singaporean police; there is speculation that some of those explosives were used in Bali bombing; photo (L)

2002-10-17.title: World Briefing | Americas: Brazil: Gangsters Attack Governor's Palace
2002-10-17.abstract: Following a failed raid to free drug lords held prisoner, criminal gangs attack Gov's Palace, shopping center and police in Rio de Janeiro; Gov Benedita da Silva stresses importance of Brazil's fight against organized crime (S)

2002-10-17.title: 'Quiet' Finland, a Bomb, and a Freaky Subculture
2002-10-17.abstract: Bombing at shopping center in Vantaa, Finland, startles peaceful country that has been largely immune to kind of violence plaguing much of rest of world; seven people died including bomber, 19-year-old student Petri Gerdt; unidentified friend of Gerdt's offers information indicating that Gerdt may have been unwitting victim of his own homemade bomb; police learn that Gerdt spent months discussing bomb-making techniques with other enthusiasts on Finnish Internet Web site devoted to bombs and explosives; site has been shut down by its sponsor, computer magazine called Mikrobitti, but backup copy is immediately posted again by high-school student; law enforcement officials say among most worrying things about bombing are ease with which forum was set up and information it contains, including data from participants' home explosives experiments; say they may never fully understand reason behind Gerdt's actions; photos; map (M)

2002-10-17.title: Oil Prize, Past and Present, Ties Russia to Iraq
2002-10-17.abstract: Russian diplomats participating in United Nations debate on Iraq are ever mindful of Russian oil companies' extensive interests in Iraqi oil; about 10 Russian companies have development agreements with Iraq; very little oil is being produced now at fields because of UN embargo; Russian companies worry that if military action by United States topples Saddam Hussein, it will also break their own hold on potentially lucrative oil fields (M)

2002-10-18.title: Indonesian Finance Chief Warns Of Need for More Foreign Aid
2002-10-18.abstract: Indonesian Finance Min Boediono warns that deadly bomb blast in Bali will force Indonesian government deeper into debt, raising cost to international community of keeping country afloat; Indonesia relied on foreign aid to recover from Asian financial crisis of 1997 and 1998; in addition to $5 billion economic rescue package administered by International Monetary Fund, it counts on billions of dollars a year in deficit financing from international donors, including World Bank, US and Japan (M)

2002-10-18.title: On the Iraq Issue: Comments by the Council Members Holding a Veto
2002-10-18.abstract: Excerpts from statements by five permanent members of e United Nations Security Council, and by Israel and Palestinians, on Iraq; photo (M)

2002-10-18.title: North Korea's Revelations Could Derail Normalization, Its Neighbors Say
2002-10-18.abstract: Japanese and South Korean officials say North Korea's self-confessed pursuit of nuclear weapons could derail efforts to normalize relations and jeopardize promise of billions of dollars in economic aid; some analysts say North Korea hopes to drive wedge between US and its two closest regional allies, Japan and South Korea, which have been comparatively friendly with North Korea; but Japan and South Korea close ranks with US, protesting North Korean revelation; photos (M)

2002-10-18.title: Palestinians And Israelis Clash in Gaza; At Least 6 Die
2002-10-18.abstract: Israeli tanks battling Palestinian gunmen fire cannon and machine guns into densely populated Rafah refugee camp in Gaza Strip, killing at least 6 people and wounding 60 others; Palestinian leadership callskillings 'new massacre'; latest bloodshed comes days after Bush administration expresses deep concern over significant rise in Palestinian civilian casualties during Israeli military operations; Israeli Army says its tanks retuned fire after anti-tank rocket fired by gunmen in camp hit armored bulldozer that was building protective earthwork around army position; says terrorists use Palestinian population as human shield; no Israeli casualties are reported; photo (M)

2002-10-18.title: Nightclub Blasts Leave Carefree Crowd Less So
2002-10-18.abstract: Young travelers who flock to Bali from around world are shocked by terrorist bombing of two nightclubs packed with partygoers (M)

2002-10-19.title: Muckraking Governor Slain By Sniper on Moscow Street
2002-10-19.abstract: Valentin Tsvetkov, governor of Russian provice of Magadan, is shot dead by sniper in Moscow; investigators and fellow politicians suspect that Tsvetkov, who had reputation of reformer, ran afoul of criminal interests; Magadan is known for its lucrative -- and, many say, corrupt -- gold-mining and fishing industries; photo (M)

2002-10-19.title: Indonesian Suspect Agrees to Questioning, Then Collapses
2002-10-19.abstract: Abu Bakar Bashi, Indonesian Muslim cleric suspected of having ties to Al Qaeda and role in several terrorist operations, agrees to go to Jakarta for questioning, but then collapses and is admitted to hospital in Solo; US and other countries, notably Australia and Singapore, have urged government not only to question Bashir but to arrest him and to declare Jemaah Islamiyah, network he leads, a terrorist organization, but governent resisted taking action until car bombing in Bali; photos (M)

2002-10-19.title: Nuclear Issue In North Korea Colors a Race In the South
2002-10-19.abstract: Conservatives hopeful of ousting South Korea's ruling party in presidential election in December seize upon North Korea's reported admission of secret nuclear weapons program to criticize Pres Kim Dae Jung and his policies; Lee Hoi Chang, conservative candidate for president, accuses government of concealing North's revelation for 13 days after learning of it from James A Kelly, US envoy; Roh Moo Hyun, governing party candidate, says Kelly's report should not be used to attack government's policy on North or as political tool (M)

2002-10-19.title: News of Ex-G.I. in North Korea Only Deepens Mystery
2002-10-19.abstract: News that Charles Robert Jenkins, former US Army sergeant, is husband of Japanese woman kidnapped by North Koreans and recently allowed to visit her home island of Sado, renews debate over whether he defected to North Korea in 1965, as Pentagon says he did, or was captured by North Koreans, as his family maintains; he declined to join his wife, Hitomi Soga, on her visit to Japan, fearing arrest (M)

2002-10-19.title: An Italian Official's Blunt Words Set Off Euro-Mayhem
2002-10-19.abstract: Romano Prodi, former Italian prime minister and now president of European Commission, says 1997 Stability and Growth Pact is stupid because of its rigidity and European Union needs more intelligent framework for single currency; remarks are met with varied levels of shock, rage, disbelief and delight; pact, which requires each of 12 governments that have adopted euro to hold its deficit to less than 3 percent of gross national product each year and to balance its budget by 2004, has been violated by Portugal and Germany (M)

2002-10-20.title: Negotiators Seeking a Deal Retreat Behind Closed Doors
2002-10-20.abstract: UN Security Council powers discuss Iraq behind closed doors as issue which US hoped to move smartly along turns into round robin of talks and pitched battle of wills with France; Prime Min Jacques Chirac considers compromise offer reportedly postponing war authorization until after weapons inspectors have done their work; photo (M)

2002-10-20.title: Iraqi Disputes Charge by Bush That Baghdad Has Qaeda Ties
2002-10-20.abstract: Iraqi Deputy Prime Min Tariq Aziz says his government has no links with Al Qaeda or Osama bin Laden as Pres Bush alleges; says Iraq's socialist party does not condone religious fundamentalism, and that UN arms inspectors will not find weapons of mass destruction (M)

2002-10-20.title: U.S. TO WITHDRAW FROM ARMS ACCORD WITH NORTH KOREA
2002-10-20.abstract: Bush administration will scrap 1994 arms control accord with North Korea that has provided Western energy aid in return for North's promise to freeze development of nuclear weapons; North Korea has admitted pursuing covert nuclear program; White House has debated ending accord, although some aides warn step could to lead to even more nuclear violations; will caution North not to remove material under international supervision at Yongbyon reactor site; immediate effect is halting of annual shipments of 500,000 tons of fuel, as US demands North abandon all nuclear weapons programs or face near-total economic isolation; US will also urge South Korea and Japan to suspend multibillion-dollar project to provide modern nuclear power to North; photo of US envoy James A Kelly; some officials fear tempting North Koreans into rapid breakout on producing weapons, but Pres Bush believes Clinton administration accord is fatally flawed (M)

2002-10-20.title: As Bodies Start Coming Home, Australians Criticize Leaders
2002-10-20.abstract: First bodies from Bali nightclub bombing are sent home as angry Australians question their government for not taking terrorism warnings seriously enough; estimated 200 Australians died in attack; just as pointed is debate over Prime Min John Howard's strong support for Bush administration's campaign against Iraq; critics say pro-American policy is dragging Australia into fights far from home (M)

2002-10-20.title: From Its Palaces, Iraq's View Is of a World Filled With Allies
2002-10-20.abstract: Iraqi leaders confronting possibility of new war with US seem convinced that, this time, world is on their side; Saddam Hussein's inner circle has also taken heart from US inability to win UN backing for resolution threatening military action, in contrast to 1991 when more than 30 nations joined coalition to trounce Iraqi occupation forces in Kuwait; Iraqi leaders say France and Russia are proving 'friendship' for Iraq; Iraqis also believe they have deftly manipulated world opinion with claims that 1.7 million people have died because of UN sanctions; Hussein, whose rambling inaugural speech features theme of American isolation, may have to struggle to grasp reality of outside world, given his personal isolation and fondness for compliant voices (M)

2002-10-21.title: Irish Vote for a Wider Union, and Europe Celebrates
2002-10-21.abstract: Irish voters endorse expansion of European Union, and diplomats and officials across continent celebrate with some relief; ballot reverses Irish vote last year to reject enlargement plan; in latest voting, about 48 percent of registered voters turn out and as much as 73 percent vote in favor in some districts; outcome ends suspenseful vigil for countries waiting to see if they would be allowed into union: Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Hungary, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Slovenia, Malta and Cyprus; next crucial consequence of Irish ballot is that people in applicant countries must also vote in referendums; sentiment against wealthy European Union has been rising in some Central and Eastern European countries; photos; map (M)

2002-10-21.title: BUSH TEAM URGES BOLD INSPECTIONS OF IRAQ'S ARSENAL
2002-10-21.abstract: Bush administration presses harder than ever for United Nations inspections of Iraq's weapons program to be carried out quickly and forcefully, to avoid any delay that could postpone retaliation for Iraq's noncompliance; delays could occur if inspectors take their time in choosing hundreds of sites or visit places that yield little conclusive evidence of Iraq's weapons programs; major factor in need for speed is military officials' claim that optimal time for using troops in Iraq is winter months, before heat and sand storms set in; negotiations to hammer out Security Council resolution are said to be close to fruition; goal of US is to have resolution refer to unspecified 'consequences'--which could be interpreted as use of force--if Iraq fails to comply with inspections and disarmament demands; US would implicitly accept idea of role by Security Council in determining what consequences should be; diplomats say US wants resolution to ensure that inspections themselves are aggressive; say some in Bush administration want inspectors to include American officials who can point them to right places (M)

2002-10-21.title: News Analysis; North Korea's Confession: Why?
2002-10-21.abstract: Analysis of possible reasons why North Korean officials decided to acknowledge existence of secret nuclear weapons program in violation of 1994 accord; experts say decision making in North Korea is driven by impulse for survival amid ever constricting options; country needs aid from Japan and China, which both object to its nuclear weapons (M)

2002-10-21.title: Korean Negotiators Shiver at Chilly Talks
2002-10-21.abstract: Atmosphere is chilly at long-scheduled meeting between officials from North Korean and South Korea to discuss reconciliation; United States is seeking support for tough diplomatic and economic stance toward North Korea following its admission that it violated accord banning nuclear weapons programs; South Korean delegate Jeong Se Hyun says warm inter-Korean relations would be difficult if international relations are frosty; photo (M)

2002-10-21.title: Why did free diver die?: Tragic plunge to the limits
2002-10-21.abstract: Push your limits too far in most sports and you lose. Push your limits too far in free diving, Audrey Mestre's sport, and you die.

2002-10-22.title: World Briefing | Africa: Eritrea: Dispute With U.S.
2002-10-22.abstract: Eritrean government claims that arrest of two nationals working at US Embassy was result of intrusion into Eritrea's internal affairs; US State Department spokesman Richard A Boucher cites arrests as evidence of continuing crackdown on human rights in Eritrea (S)

2002-10-22.title: Iraqi City Seen as Powder Keg If Hussein Were to Be Ousted
2002-10-22.abstract: Iraqi government has displaced or relocated nearly million Iraqis in last three decades, majority of them Kurds; report by Brookings Institution says this has created deep regional and ethnic fractures that could erupt into civil war if Pres Saddam Hussein is deposed; says Kurds whose villages were razed or mined by Iraqi military are living in ramshackle communities, and any effort by them to regain control of fertile, oil-rich northern city of Kirkuk could set off conflict; report underscores rising tide of concern among Iraq's neighbors, international aid groups and foreign policy experts about chaos that could be unleashed if US forcefully deposes Hussein (M)

2002-10-22.title: U.S. REFINES PLANS FOR WAR IN CITIES
2002-10-22.abstract: American military adopts new strategy in training for attack on Baghdad that will seek to avoid house-to-house combat in its hostile streets; new plan calls for isolating city and taking control of it by seizing or destroying Saddam Hussein's pillars of power with such decisive force that Iraqis' will to fight collapses; commanders say any fighting in Baghdad or other Iraqi cities could result in thousands of American casualties and even more civiilian deaths; they may have no choice should Hussein and his die-hard adherents choose to make Baghdad their last stand; even now Republican Guard units are digging in around Baghdad; new American military strategy described in detail; photos (L)

2002-10-22.title: Abrupt Amnesty At Iraqi Prisons: A Joy for Many, Grief for Some
2002-10-22.abstract: Amnesty with which Pres Saddam Hussein emptied Iraq's jails has shaken country to its core; mobs of relatives storm cellblocks of prisons to liberate as many as 10,000 captives; some relatives roam prison compounds looking for prisoners who are probably long since dead, victims of secret executions; scene described; photos (L)

2002-10-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: 300 Illegal Immigrants Captured
2002-10-22.abstract: Two ships overcrowded with illegal immigrants are seized off coast of Sicily; over 300 people from Somalia, Sierra Leone and Sudan and Turkish Kurds are detained (S)

2002-10-23.title: Climate Talks Will Shift Focus From Emissions
2002-10-23.abstract: Latest round of international talks on global warming opens in New Delhi, with focus more on adapting to changes than on cutting emissions of gases; shift follows Bush administration's rejection of Kyoto protocol, which has little effect without US acceptance (M)

2002-10-23.title: Havel Denies Telephoning U.S. On Iraq Meeting
2002-10-23.abstract: Czech Pres Vaclav Havel's spokesman denies The New York Times report that Havel called senior Bush administration officials to explain that Czech authorities have no proof that Sept 11 hijacker Mohamed Atta met in Prague with Iraqi spy in April 2001 (S)

2002-10-23.title: Chechnya's Wine Industry Clings to Remaining Roots
2002-10-23.abstract: Chechnya's wine industry, which once flourished along dry sandy valley of Terek River, did not produce world-class vintages but is remembered nostalgically by Chechens and Russians as symbol of more peaceful times; post-Soviet government, restoring Muslim traditions, stopped supporting wineries and many were destroyed during civil war; only four producers are now left of industry that once accounted for thirty percent of republic's domestic product (S)

2002-10-23.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-10-23.abstract: JAPAN: Kidnapping Victims May Stay Longer; CHINA: Leadership Shuffle Starts;.

2002-10-23.title: No Retaliation From Israel After Bombing
2002-10-23.abstract: Israel does not retaliate for bus bombing that killed 14 passengers, in nod to US concerns that action could disrupt plans for action against Iraq; Asst Sec William Burns will arrive for talks with Israelis and Palestinians on 'road map' toward negotiations; officials say terrorists took advantage of eased curfew to drive car bomb into Israel; Islamic Jihad says bombers came from Jenin; Israel has recently pulled back forces from city (M)

2002-10-24.title: Helsinki Journal; Jacks? Dolls? Yo-Yos? No, They Want Cellphones
2002-10-24.abstract: Helsinki, Finland, home to cellphone giant Nokia, is one of most cell-happy cities in world; estimated 92 percent of its households have at least one, if not several cellphones; teenagers have long used cellphones, but now market is undergoing rapid expansiondownward in age; cellphone retailers are making pitch to children as young as 7; at one elementary school in Helsinki, second graders all want Nokia cellphone, accessorized with personal logo on screen; photos (M)

2002-10-24.title: 2 Yugoslav Aides Are Fired for Sales of Fighter-Jet Parts to Iraq
2002-10-24.abstract: Yugoslavia fires Deputy Defense Min Ivan Djokic and Jovan Cekovic, director of state trading company Yugoimport, as result of NATO's accusations that Bosnian Serb company Orao exported military hardware to Iraq via Belgrade in violation of United Nations arms embargo; diplomats say NATO soldiers raided Orao factory in Sept and found document telling staff members to remain in Iraq and remove all traces of company's involvement there (M)

2002-10-24.title: Chechens Seize Moscow Theater, Taking as Many as 600 Hostages
2002-10-24.abstract: Forty Chechen guerrillas wearing masks and camouflage and firing automatic rifles storm into crowded theater in Moscow and take up to 600 hostages, threatening to blow up building; witnesses say guerrillas have wired themselves and three-story music hall with explosives and appear to be preparing for assault by police; Russia's domestic intelligence agency says attackers are demanding end to war in Chechnya as well as large amount of money; Moscow police officials deny that negotiations have been opened with guerrillas or that ransom demand has been made; gunshots ring out periodically from building and there are reports that one man has been taken to hospital and that grenade was tossed at police; truckloads of police officers and soldiers surround building and block off streets; Pres Vladimir V Putin cancels planned visit to Germany and orders alite Alpha counterterrorist squad to scene; clearly terrified hostage, speaking from inside theater on cellphone, warns that any attempt by police or soldiers to enter building would end in carnage; guerrillas release unknown number of hostages, mostly children, at outset of seizure; photos; map (L)

2002-10-24.title: France and Germany Clinch Landmark Deal to Enlarge E.U.
2002-10-24.abstract: France and Germany reached a landmark agreement today to curb European Union farm spending, unlocking the final phase of negotiations to enlarge the 15-nation bloc.

2002-10-24.title: Japan Seeks Amnesty for Ex-U.S. Soldier Living in North Korea
2002-10-24.abstract: Japanese government official asks United States to grant special amnesty to Charles Robert Jenkins, former American serviceman who is married to Japanese woman living in North Korea; Jenkins disappeared while leading Army patrol on South Korean side of demilitarized zone in 1965 and later took part in radio broadcasts to American troops from Pyongyang; Pentagon lists him as deserter (M)

2002-10-25.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: General Is Suspended
2002-10-25.abstract: A major general who told Parliament that the defense minister had ignored his warnings of a North Korean plan to attack a South Korean boat has been suspended for a month. After five South Korean sailors were killed in a North Korean attack on a Southern patrol boat in the Yellow Sea on June 29, Maj. Gen. Han Cheol Yong made his assertion about the response of the defense minister at the time, Kim Dong Shim. A defense ministry legal officer said General Han had ''failed to protect classified information and breached military discipline.'' The general said he would appeal.   Don Kirk (NYT)

2002-10-25.title: Terrorism Panel Says U.S. Is Still Unready
2002-10-25.abstract: Despite months of preparations and the spending of millions of dollars, the United States remains extremely vulnerable to a terrorist attack, particularly at seaports, power plants and oil refineries, a panel of security experts has concluded.    In a report being released today, the panel contends that police officials still have little access to intelligence on terror suspects, that only a tiny fraction of vessels entering American ports are inspected and that most emergency workers are ill equipped to handle biowar attacks.

2002-10-25.title: Japan's Economy Czar Caught in Bitter Revolt
2002-10-25.abstract: Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi's hand-picked economic czar Heizo Takenaka comes under fresh attack over his plan for radical action to clean up country's banking crisis; four opposition parties in Parliament submit motion to censure Takenaka for 'irresponsible' handing of financial matters; ruling coalition will probably defeat censure motion, but conservatives in ruling party are also working to undercut him by drafting alternative for dealing with bankers' immense problems with bad debts (M)

2002-10-25.title: U.S. outlines shift in criteria for providing development aid
2002-10-25.abstract: PARIS:—  The head of the U.S. Agency for International Development this week has been explaining plans to restrict additional U.S. government aid to all but about 15 countries with solid democratic credentials and promising economies.

2002-10-25.title: Ex-Tennis Star Given 2 Years' Probation
2002-10-25.abstract: Former German tennis star Boris Becker is sentenced to two years' probation and fined nearly $300,000 after being found guilty of income tax evasion in his native country (S)

2002-10-26.title: Bush and Jiang Vow to Cooperate on North Korea Issue
2002-10-26.abstract: Pres Bush and Pres Jiang Zemin of China, at Bush's ranch in Crawford, Texas, publicly vow to work together to persuade North Korea to abandon its newest nuclear weapons project, though they carefully wend their way around divisive question of whther China would take part in Washington's emerging strategy of economically isolating North unless it complies; meeting at ranch is initially planned as largely ceremonial farewell visit for Jiang as he gives up China's presidency; photo (M)

2002-10-26.title: U.S. Raises Pressure on Russia and France for Iraq Resolution
2002-10-26.abstract: United States presses in United Nations for rapid approval of its tough resolution to disarm Iraq while France and Russia present their own informal texts designed to moderate threat of military attack (M)

2002-10-26.title: Europeans Smooth Over Differences, but Tensions Remain
2002-10-26.abstract: Fifteen leaders of European Union arrive at agreement on how to pay for 10 countries invited to join union, only after bitter haggling over money and deferral of some decisions for years; subsidies to be given to new members will reach same level as existing member states in 10 years; new members are Poland, Hungary, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Slovenia, Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, Cyprus and Malta (M)

2002-10-26.title: RUSSIA RECAPTURES THEATER AFTER CHECHEN REBEL GROUP BEGINS TO EXECUTE HOSTAGES
2002-10-26.abstract: Russian special forces burst into Moscow theater held by Chechen guerrillas after rebels begin killing some of more than 600 hostages they had seized; government forces pour into theater in pre-dawn raid after panicked hostages run out, evidently as guerrillas begin to carry out their threats to kill hostages; it is not immediately clear how many people are killed in police raid, which begins with series of thunderous explosions, punctuated by bursts of gunfire, then quiet; one hostage says Russian forces filled theater with gas that put those inside to sleep; it is not clear what substance was; Russia's official television network shows film of scene inside theater, including grim scenes of raid's bloody aftermath and bodies of several guerrillas; at last 70 ambulances speed in and out of area, ferrying wounded to hospitals; 10 hostages are reported killed, but many more are treated inside theater and at hospitals; toll could rise; several guerrillas are reported to have escaped, and two are captured on street outside theater; photos; diagrams (L)

2002-10-26.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-10-26.abstract: EUROPEAN UNION SHIFT ON RUSSIAN ENCLAVE; AEGEAN: Military Exercises Put Off; ITALY: Financier'S '82 Death Called Murder;.

2002-10-27.title: U.S. and 2 of Its Allies Warn North Korea on Atomic Arms
2002-10-27.abstract: United States, Japan and South Korea issue joint statement, Cabo San Lucas, Mexico, calling on North Korea to dismantle its clandestine nuclear weapons program 'in a prompt and verifiable manner' and warning Kim Jong Il, North Korea's leader, that his 'relations with the international community' hinge on his quick compliance with demand; Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation summit opens in Cabo San Lucas; photo of Pres Bush and Pres Vicente Fox of Mexico (M)

2002-10-27.title: Rightist Confesses to Killing Japanese Lawmaker, Police Say
2002-10-27.abstract: Japanese police say Hakusui Ito, right-wing extremist with record of assaulting members of leftist groups, has confessed to killing Koki Ishii, member of parliament; photos (S)

2002-10-27.title: The Rescue, Seen From Inside: 'We Will All Be Blown Up'
2002-10-27.abstract: Interviews with hostages in Moscow theater describe their ordeal under siege by Chechnyan guerrillas, and storming of theater by Russian special forces; photos (M)

2002-10-27.title: Polls Say Leftist Will Win Brazil's Presidency by Wide Margin
2002-10-27.abstract: Second and decisive round in Brazil0

2002-10-27.title: France Is Set to Offer the U.N. A Separate Resolution on Iraq
2002-10-27.abstract: France, in bold diplomatic challenge to United States, announces that it might formally introduce its own resolution on disarming Iraq at United Nations Security Council; French Foreign Min Dominique de Villepin emphasizes that France is willing to use draft resolution formally presented to Security Council by United States as basis for agreement among its 15 members (M)

2002-10-28.title: Pacific Summitry in an Age of Peril: The U.S. Is Mr. Big
2002-10-28.abstract: Twenty-one nations of Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation end summit in Cabo San Lucas, Mexico, with formal statements underscoring how US campaign against terrorism is influencing global agenda; issue series of promises and proposls to make rest of world as secure from terrorists as US is trying to be; adopt measures drafted by US to heighten security and to monitor charities and money-transfer offices suspected of financing terrorist organizations; issue declaration acknowledging that terrorism threatens free, open and prosperous economies as well as international peace, security and economic sustainability (M)

2002-10-28.title: Music and Victory in the Streets of Brazil
2002-10-28.abstract: Tens of thousands of supporters of Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva throng main boulevard in Sao Paulo to celebrate his victory in Brazil's presidential election; photo (M)

2002-10-28.title: Turkey, in the Middle, Grows More Worried Every Day About a U.S. Attack on Iraq
2002-10-28.abstract: Turkey is still withholding wholehearted support for military action against Iraq sought by US, and discomfort of government leaders grows daily; government is concerned that war could worsen already dire economy and inflict new problems of refugees and Kurdish separatism; Turkish officials, analysts and diplomats say real issue is not whether Turkey will go along in operation against Iraq but how to strike balance between preserving long and broad friendship with US and its own strategic interests; photo (M)

2002-10-28.title: Showdown Over Japanese Banking Reform
2002-10-28.abstract: Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi comes under increasing pressure on bank reform; Economics Min Heizo Takenaka and Bank of Japan governor Masaru Hayami advocate shock therapy despite economic pains, while political elite that has held near-monopoly for half a century fears reforms may push many of ruling party's biggest corporate donors out of business; bad loans have mired nation in depression, and price deflation has become so embedded in economy that loans are going sour faster than banks can write them off; banks are cutting lending to cover losses, suffocating healthy companies; chart (M)

2002-10-28.title: Iraq Offers West's Reporters A Kinder, Gentler Face
2002-10-28.abstract: Iraq's Information Ministry launches charm offensive to convince Western news media that Iraq is not hostile to Western news coverage; flurry of enhanced amiability seems part of wider effort by Pres Saddam Hussein to present more moderate image to world in face of American war threats; comes amid signs of uncertainty among high-level officials over how far Iraq should go in its efforts at describing itself as something other than repressive state; follows criticisms of ministry by Hussein's son Uday for overreacting in its handling of CNN, whose staff is being expelled for wide coverage of scattered street protests in Baghdad; photos (M)

2002-10-29.title: An Uneasy Wait in Oklahoma
2002-10-29.abstract: Workers at General Motors plant in Midwest City, Okla, wear red-white-and-blue ribbons for colleague Sandy Alan Booker, feared dead in siege that ended occupation of Moscow theater by Chechen rebels; he was completing preparations in Moscow to bring Russian fiancee back to US; she remains in coma in Moscow hospital (S)

2002-10-29.title: Draft Charter for Europe Points the Way to a Bigger World Role
2002-10-29.abstract: Draft constitution for enlarged European Union is unveiled in Brussels, pointing way toward creating greater European presence on world stage; seeks to raise Europe's profile by proposing common foreign, security and defense policies; examines role of European Union's foreign policy chief, currently Javier Solana, with view to elevating position to level enjoyed in US by secretary of state; suggests creating elected president for union and Congress, consisting of members of both European and national parliaments; was prepared under leadership of Valery Giscard d'Estaing, former president of France (M)

2002-10-29.title: Sharon Says He Is Ready for Early Elections Over Budget
2002-10-29.abstract: Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon, facing stiff challenge from main coalition partner over financing for Jewish settlements, says he is ready for early elections if his government unravels; Labor Party led by Defense Min Benjamin Ben-Eliezer, has threatened to vote against proposed 2003 budget if funds are not shifted from settlements to social needs; Sharon has warned vote would result in dismissal of party ministers from cabinet, forcxing early elections; photos (M)

2002-10-29.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Gujurat Plans Elections
2002-10-29.abstract: State Assembly elections will be held December 12 in Gujarat, India, despite continuing Hindu-Muslim riots (S)

2002-10-29.title: North Korea Rejects Demands to Abandon Nuclear Program
2002-10-29.abstract: North Korea rejected demands to abandon its program in the opening session of normalization talks with Japan.

2002-10-30.title: Investigators In Jordan Seek Clues in Death Of U.S. Envoy
2002-10-30.abstract: Jordan begins rounding up dozens of men with links to training camps in Afghanistan or other Islamic causes, seeking clues in killing of American diplomat Laurence Foley; security around diplomatic residences in Amman is beefed up as government seeks to buttress sense that Jordan remains country where Westerners can feel safe; newspaper editorials and columnists inveigh against killing of Foley; officials privately speak of terrorism but publicly maintain there is not yet enough evidence to make such an assertion (M)

2002-10-30.title: Vandals, With Fire and Rockets, Attack 4 Afghan Girls' Schools
2002-10-30.abstract: Vandals attack four elementary schools for girls outside Kabul, Afghanistan, firing rockets at two and burning blackboards and floor mats at two others; no one is hurt in attacks, which occur at night; this is third time small-scale arson and bomb attacks have been carried out against Afghan girls' schools over last six weeks; no one claims responsibility for attacks, but suspicions center on backers of country's former Taliban rulers, who barred girls and women from most forms of education (M)

2002-10-30.title: Pyongyang Rejects Demands to End Atom Bomb Program
2002-10-30.abstract: North Korea flatly rejects international demands that it abandon its nuclear weapons program; issues statement at opening session of talks with Japan aimed at establishing normal relations between two countries (M)

2002-10-30.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Head Of Orthodox Church Ailing
2002-10-30.abstract: Russian Orthodox Church Patriarch Aleksy II is hospitalized while visiting Russian republic of Astrakhan; photo (S)

2002-10-30.title: U.S. AND FRANCE NEAR COMPROMISE ON ACTION ON IRAQ
2002-10-30.abstract: United States and France move toward compromise on Iraq that would oblige Bush administration to consult United Nations Security Council before embarking on military action against Saddam Hussein but still leave it freedom to act alone; France and US have been arguing for six weeks over how many resolutions and what sort of triggers would be needed to open way for UN-backed military action against Iraq if that country fails to comply with expected new UN demands for destruction of its chemical and biological weapons; US has balked at France's insistence on two resolutions; under proposed compromise, American officials make clear that US would reserve right to lead military action against Iraq if Iraq continues to block inspections, even if Security Council does not give its approval (M)

2002-10-31.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Moscow's Population Passes 10 Million
2002-10-31.abstract: Moscow Mayor Yuri M Luzhkov asserts Moscow's population has increased to 10.4 million; figures are preliminary numbers from recent Russian census (S)

2002-10-31.title: Party With Islamic Roots Likely to Win Turkish Vote
2002-10-31.abstract: Justice and Development, untested party with Islamic roots, is expected to win in Turkey's Nov 3 parliamentary elections; that prospect scares many in moderate Muslim nation, while others see it as crucial test of how well democracy and greater emphasis on Islam can co-exist; polls give party as much as 30 percent of vote; party hopes to translate lead into single-party government; Turkey has zealously defended its secularism since its 1923 founding as republic; military is not expected to step in, as it has done whenever it felt state was threatened, and that is seen as measure of progress in country's long and fitful drive to full democracy; Recep Tayyip Erdogan, leader of party, is not promising to Islamicize Turkey; he and party vigorously play down party's Islamic roots; hope to focus attention on party's determination to end corruption, to revive economy and to listen to plight of ordinary Turks; photo (M)

2002-10-31.title: Japan-North Korea Talks Conclude With Deep Splits
2002-10-31.abstract: Japan and North Korea end two days of negotiations, angrily talking past each other on issues that each side depicts as major obstacles to normalized relations; Japan's insistence on visits by immediate relatives of people kidnapped by North Korea draws strong rebuke from North Korean delegates; Tokyo says economic aid will not be forthcoming until abduction issue and concerns about North Korea's nuclear weapons program are resolved; desite deep divide, two sides claims some modest progress; agree to establish panel to discuss security issues, although it is unclear whether North Korea would agree to discuss nuclear arms at that forum (M)

2002-10-31.title: SHARON COALITION SHATTERS AS LABOR QUITS OVER BUDGET
2002-10-31.abstract: Israel's Labor Party ministers resign from coalition government over their assertion that Prime Min Ariel Sharon's budget favors Israel's settlers at expense of its poor; break is almost certain to drive Israeli government to right; it is first attempt by moderately leftist party to find political voice and present coherent alternative to Sharon since his crushing victory almost two years ago over his predecessor, Labor candidate Ehud Barak; loss of Labor's 25 seats in 120-seat Parliament leaves Sharon with minority government of 55 seats; he faces three basic options: he can try to govern with his remaining, right-of-center minority, which would leave him vulnerable to possible no-confidence vote; he can try to bring in ultra-right factions that would give him bare majority but also heighten risk that his government would appear extremist; or he can ask Pres Moshe Katsav to dissolve Parliament and call elections within 90 days, nine months ahead of schedule; allies say Sharon is likely to pursue first or second option for now; Defense Min Benjamin Ben-Eliezer, explaining his decision to leave government, says it is time to show Palestinians 'diplomatic horizon'; but most likely result of his departure will be to empower hard-line groups most opposed to dealing with Palestinians; photo; chart (M)

2002-10-31.title: Chechen Leader Arrested In Denmark Is a Quarry Of Putin's Terrorist Hunt
2002-10-31.abstract: Senior Chechen leader Akhmed Zakayev is arrested and jailed in Copenhagen on Russian charges that he was involved in Moscow theater siege; seizure signals that Pres Vladimir Putin's vow to apprehend Chechen separatists in American-style, worldwide war on terror is winning friends beyond his borders; Zakayev is in Copenhagen to attend so-called World Chechen Congress, which Russia had urged Denmark to block; conference had drafted statements calling for negotiations to end war in Chechnya; Zakayev's arrest is major coup for Putin; Danish judge orders Zakayev held until Nov 12 because of risk he might flee while question of his extradition to Russia is settled; photo (M)
